gloss: to renounce / ward off by oath
definition: To abjure is to renounce something under oath — a faith, an error, a spirit's claim — formally forswearing what one once held or what threatens one. Where to conjure binds a power by swearing it in, to abjure repudiates by swearing it out; it is the oath run in reverse, disavowal made binding.
history: The root is legal Latin *abiurare*, 'to deny on oath' (*ab-*, 'away from', + *iurare*, 'to swear') — the exact inverse of conjuring, which binds by an oath sworn together. The turn was ecclesiastical: to abjure became the courtroom act of recanting heresy or witchcraft. In Tudor Wells, one Katherine Love stood before the vicar-general to 'curse, damn, and abjure' her charms; Menocchio's inquisitors closed his trial with a lengthy abjuration. So the word carried a double charge — the exorcist's *I abjure you* commanding a demon out, and the penitent's oath surrendering forbidden knowledge. Magic keeps the warding sense: abjuration is the school of protective, dispelling spells.

def_sources.label: Wright & Klaassen, Everyday Magicians (abjuration formula, Wells)
Ginzburg, The Cheese and the Worms (Menocchio's abjuration)

etymology.origin: Latin abiurare 'to deny on oath' (ab- + iurare 'to swear')
etymology.note: The anti-conjuration: where you conjure by swearing an oath, you abjure by un-swearing one. Magic runs on oaths, made and forsworn.
